title: Thick, strong sediment subduction along south-central Chile and its role in great earthquakes
abstract: Using a seismic survey conducted in 2017 with a 15.15-km receiver array, shallow structures along south-central Chile are imaged over ~1000 km of margin length. Relative to other sediment-dominated subduction zones where sediment is typically accreted at the toe, an unusually large percentage of the thick trench sediments are consistently subducted beneath the slope. The well-drained, strong sediments are linked to the occurrence of great earthquakes in this region. The subduction of the strong, well-drained, thick sediment layer creates favorable conditions for a smooth plate interface that generates particularly powerful earthquakes, distinguishing this margin from other subduction zones globally.

location.description: South-central Chile margin from ~30°S to 44°S, ~1000 km long seismic survey conducted in Jan–Feb 2017 (MGL1701 CEVICHE)

seismic_lines.name: MGL1701 CEVICHE 2017 South-central Chile Survey (~1000 km margin-parallel and trench-perpendicular profiles)

acquisition.vessel: R/V Marcus G. Langseth (MGL1701 — CEVICHE cruise, Jan–Feb 2017)

data.name: MGL1701 (CEVICHE) MCS Reflection Data — South-central Chile 2017
data.description: MCS reflection shot data from the CEVICHE experiment (MGL1701, Jan–Feb 2017) along ~1000 km of south-central Chile margin (30°–44°S). 1212-channel, 15.1-km streamer, targeting 2015 Illapel, 2010 Maule, and 1960 Valdivia earthquake source regions. Records consistent subduction of thick trench sediments. Cruise DOI: 10.7284/907175.

data.name: MGL1701 Bathymetry:Swath (Multibeam EM122) — CEVICHE cruise 2017
data.description: Raw multibeam swath bathymetry data (1,753 files, ~201 GB, Kongsberg EM122) from the 2017 CEVICHE (MGL1701) cruise, offshore south-central Chile 30°–44°S. Released August 2022.

data.name: MGL1701 Gravity (BGM-3 Gravimeter) — CEVICHE cruise 2017
data.description: Raw underway gravimeter data from the 2017 CEVICHE (MGL1701) cruise, offshore south-central Chile.

data.name: MGL1701 Magnetics (G-882 Magnetometer) — CEVICHE cruise 2017
data.description: Raw magnetometer data from the 2017 CEVICHE (MGL1701) cruise, offshore south-central Chile.

data.name: MGL1701 Navigation/Positions — CEVICHE cruise 2017
data.description: Primary navigation (GPS positions, 253 MB) from the 2017 CEVICHE (MGL1701) cruise, archived via R2R.

data.name: MGL1701 Seismic Navigation — Shot Time (ASCII, Processed) — CEVICHE cruise 2017
data.description: Processed seismic shot time navigation data from the MGL1701 CEVICHE cruise, offshore south-central Chile. For use when processing the MCS reflection data.

processing.summary: Multichannel seismic reflection data acquired in 2017 along ~1000 km of south-central Chile margin (30°–44°S) during MGL1701 CEVICHE cruise, using a 15.15 km long receiver array (1212 channels, 6600 in³ source). Standard MCS processing applied including multiple suppression, velocity analysis, NMO, stack, and post-stack migration. The long streamer provides extended offset and improved imaging at depth. Results reveal consistent subduction of thick trench sediments rather than frontal accretion, providing a structural explanation for the region's propensity for great earthquakes.
workflow: 1. Data acquisition (MGL1701 CEVICHE, Jan–Feb 2017, 15.15 km streamer)
10. Interpretation of accretionary vs. erosive processes
processing.notes: Bronze open access manuscript available. Exceptional dataset: 15.15 km streamer covering ~1000 km of south-central Chile in 2017. UTIG-OSU-Chile collaboration. Cruise MGL1701 (CEVICHE) confirmed; DOI 10.7284/907175. Note: a prior version of this record incorrectly listed the MGL1610 MGDS URL for this 2017 dataset — corrected.

analysis_notes: Bronze open access manuscript available via ScienceDirect. Abstract confirmed from Semantic Scholar summary. 2017 cruise confirmed as MGL1701 (CEVICHE project, Nathan Bangs + Anne Trehu PIs, Jan 10–Feb 18 2017, cruise DOI 10.7284/907175). 15.15 km streamer length explicitly stated in paper. Previously the data URL incorrectly pointed to MGL1610 — corrected to MGL1701 resources. Arnulf co-authorship confirms UT Institute for Geophysics involvement.
